title: A Special Diet Helps People with Crohn's Disease Feel Better
summary: Scientists studied a special diet to help people with Crohn's disease, a sickness that affects the stomach and intestines. In a big test, they had 97 patients try a diet that mimics fasting for five days each month, where they ate fewer calories and plant-based foods. The rest of the month, they ate normally. After three months, about two-thirds of those on the diet felt better, with less pain and other symptoms, while fewer than half in the control group improved. This diet could help doctors give better advice on what to eat to manage Crohn's disease, which is important because many patients ask about food but there hasn't been much research on it. The study was published in a science journal and involved researchers from Stanford Medicine.

keywords.explanation: a sickness that causes inflammation in the stomach and intestines, leading to pain and other problems
the food and drinks a person eats regularly
a special eating plan where you eat fewer calories for a few days to act like fasting
units of energy in food that our bodies use

questions.correct_answer: It helps doctors give better dietary advice

background_read: Crohn's disease is a long-term condition that affects the digestive system, causing inflammation and symptoms like stomach pain and diarrhea. Many people with this disease wonder what foods they should eat to feel better, but doctors haven't had much research to guide them. This study tested a special diet that mimics fasting, where patients eat fewer calories for a short time, to see if it improves their health. Understanding diets can help manage chronic illnesses without always needing strong medicines.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers from Stanford Medicine and patients with Crohn's disease
WHAT: A study testing a fasting mimicking diet to improve symptoms
WHERE: Across the United States in a clinical trial
WHY: To provide better dietary advice for managing Crohn's disease

perspectives.description: Doctors and scientists see this as a way to offer evidence-based dietary recommendations to patients.
